title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kelley has more adventures ahead By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: February 20th, 2005 Last Modified: February 20th, 2005 at 03:57 AM Anchorage's Tim Kelley has done so much -- climbed Mount McKinley, skied the Iditarod Trail to Nome, crossed the Brooks Range a time or two, bagged many of the peaks around Prince William Sound, skied down Sleeping Lady, designed some of his own outdoor gear and won more nordic ski races than anyone can remember. But the former member of the U.S. Nordic Ski Team isn't showing any signs of slowing down. When Kelley looks at the crags and corners of Alaska he has yet to conquer, opportunity stares back. He still bubbles with youthful enthusiasm about the Alaska wilderness, trains like a kid and yearns for what tomorrow might bring instead of thin…

summary: Dog mushing roundup: Quest half finished for Skagway musher Daily News staff Published: February 18th, 2005 Last Modified: February 18th, 2005 at 03:30 AM Seven teams have now reached Dawson City, Yukon, during the fifth day of mushing in the Yukon Quest International Sled Dog Race. Hugh Neff of Skagway was the first musher into the Dawson City checkpoint at 11:21 a.m. Neff will collect the Dawson City award, four ounces of placer gold, presented to the first musher to the old Gold Rush capital if he completes the 1,000-mile race to Fairbanks. Kasilof's Jon Little was the second team to arrive at 12:17 p.m. Gerry Willomitzer and William Kleedehn rolled into Dawson City at the same time at 1:20 p.m. and share third place. Sebastian Schnuelle of Whitehorse, Yukon, arrived at 3:40 p.m., nearl…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Ideal places to get kids hooked on fishing SPECIAL SETTINGS: Alaska has abundance of areas that are perfect learning environments. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: September 2nd, 2004 Last Modified: September 15th, 2004 at 10:53 AM LOWER SUMMIT LAKE -- They were little fish for little girls, native goldenfin Dolly Varden mostly -- a diminutive subspecies found in the brooks and high-country tarns of the Chugach and Kenai mountains. A few small rainbow trout were among them. By little I mean six inches long with "trophies" topping 10 inches. But what the fish lacked in size they made up for in abundance, pluck and a willingness to strike No. 12 dry flies of all types. Besides, as far as Natalie, 11, and sister Stephanie, 8, were concerned Tuesday evening, size was not the point.…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Alaska sports digest: Willomitzer grabs Quest lead Daily news staff Published: February 16th, 2005 Last Modified: February 16th, 2005 at 04:51 AM Gerry Willomitzer has passed all 21 mushers in the Yukon Quest International Sled Dog Race field. Willomitzer was the last musher to head down the trail when the Quest began Sunday in Whitehorse. By Tuesday morning, he was the first musher into Pelly Crossing, 17 minutes ahead of fellow German musher William Kleedehn, one of the favorites who broke his leg and withdrew from last year's race. Pelly Crossing is 263 miles from Whitehorse. The race is tightly contested. Seven mushers, including Willomitzer, left Pelly Crossing within less than two hours of one another Tuesday night, led by Sam Perrino of Yellowknife, Northwest Territories, and Jon Li…

summary: Tesoro Iron Dog snowmobile race: Riders wallow toward Nome STORM: Trail keeps getting buried; some riders run out of gas. trail breakers find it tough sledding. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: February 16th, 2005 Last Modified: February 16th, 2005 at 04:50 AM As the snow-plagued Tesoro Iron Dog snowmobile race inched toward Nome Tuesday, leaders Ken Lee and Tracey Brassard were sitting in the Yukon River village of Kaltag waiting for other racers to catch up and hoping for good news about the trail ahead. Reports on the condition of the 90-mile stretch from Kaltag to Unalakleet on the Bering Sea were not good. summary: Lynne Curry: Deadline pressure strategy is lacking LYNNE CURRY MANAGEMENT Published: January 24th, 2005 Last Modified: January 25th, 2005 at 02:39 AM Q. Last night I worked until 2 a.m. to finish a project due this morning. The project I finally turned in wasn't great because I couldn't get all the information I needed from my co-workers over the weekend. I know I should have started this assignment a week ago, but I kept putting it off. Friday afternoon I panicked when I realized the project was due Monday morning and I wouldn't have time to finish it. So I killed a perfectly good weekend to get everything ready but the final project I turned in was incomplete. Until now, I've always felt I've done my best work under deadline pressure but lately I'm getting frustrated about what last-minu…

It specializes in X-ray systems that provide digital images, as opposed to film, custom-built for specific commercial applications. But after a while Phillips felt she wasn't doing her life's work. She took a risk four years ago and traded her nonprofit job for life as a full-time yoga teacher and massage therapist. Phillips doesn't have as much disposable income as she used to, but life is infinitely more satisfying, she said. "I don't measure success so much in money but in happiness," Phillips said, over a steaming cup of tea at Cafe del Mundo. summary: Sharing a common attraction for the building's history, the new business owners are working to turn an old hotel into Fairbanks' next mini-mall -- a difficult task in a place where many of the rooms look like they haven't been touched in nearly three decades. "In the back of my mind I always wanted to have a gift shop up here," Quist said. "I guess it's just a natural draw I have for the building." An immigrant from Greece, Quist lived in the hotel when she first arrived in Fairbanks in 1971. Her first job was working as a cocktail waitress at The Big International, and staying in the upstairs hotel was a convenient living arrangement. summary: Conoco Phillips doubles profit The Associated Press Published: January 27th, 2005 Last Modified: January 28th, 2005 at 12:31 AM HOUSTON -- Conoco Phillips, the nation's third biggest oil company, said it more than doubled its fourth-quarter income because of high crude and natural gas prices and better refining margins. Houston-based Conoco Phillips reported fourth-quarter net income of $2.43 billion for the October-December period, up from $1.02 billion a year ago. Revenues for the quarter were $40.1 billion compared with $26 billion in the fourth quarter of 2003. For the year, Conoco Phillips reported net income of $8.13 billion, or $11.60 per share, up from $4.74 billion, or $6.91 per share, in 2003. State biologist Rick Sinnott says he would rather see a few moose shot in Chugach State Park than have them starve or struck by cars. "We're not talking about reducing them to invisibility," he said. "It just seems a shame to have them starving to death." summary: The fire was on board the Kenai as it was tied up at the Valdez Marine Terminal. "They'd just got rid of the ballast water," said Amil Mathur, president of Alaska Tanker Co., which operates the Kenai on behalf of BP. Crews noticed black smoke and halted operations. All preparations to load were halted and the alarm was sounded. The fire was extinguished quickly in the 15-by-15-foot room. "We got some help from Alyeska (Pipeline Service Co.)," Mathur told the Valdez Star. summary: Entrepreneur cleans up washing nasty sports equipment By SARANA SCHELL Anchorage Daily News Published: January 26th, 2005 Last Modified: January 27th, 2005 at 03:17 AM There's a myth that hockey-gear stench is good luck. "It's not. It's bad hygiene," said Kris Gills, who got so sick of the smell of his own gear he took a hockey duffle full of foulness to Canada to try out a special washing machine invented to clean gnarly and traditionally unwashed items. It worked so well he bought one of the $90,000 gizmos and started Alaska Sport Wash two years ago. summary: Hawaii next for floating college DAMAGED: Students had a hot meal, resumed classes. By MEGAN HOLLAND Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 28, 2005) Click on photo to enlarge More than a day after a giant wave temporarily crippled a ship carrying hundreds of college students on an educational trip around the world, the vessel resumed operations at near-normal speed and was headed Thursday for Hawaii. Officials said rough sea conditions in the North Pacific that broke windows, damaged the ship's electrical system and took out its engines had subsided and life was returning to its routine.
